content:
An interview that combined data structures with GenAI concepts.

2 min read
Just now

--

AI startups are increasingly looking for software engineers who are comfortable with both traditional coding interviews and modern AI concepts.
That was exactly the case during my interview with Ema.ai, an AI startup building enterprise AI employees using multi-agent systems.
Here’s how the interview went.
Quick Summary
Role: Software Engineering Intern
Stipend: ₹60K/month
Full-Time Offer: ~₹35 LPA
Rounds:
- DSA Round
- GenAI Round

Round 1: DSA Interview
The first round was a standard DSA interview consisting of two coding questions.
Questions Asked

• DFS-based Tree Problem

• Search in Rotated Sorted Array
My Approach
I solved both questions while explaining my thought process, discussing edge cases, and walking through dry runs. We also discussed the time and space complexity of each solution before moving on.
The overall difficulty was around Easy–Medium, with the interviewer placing equal emphasis on communication and problem-solving.

Round 2: GenAI Round
The second round shifted completely towards Generative AI.
The interviewer wanted to understand how I would build and reason about a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system.
Some of the topics we discussed included:

• Design a complete RAG pipeline from user query to final response.

• How would you retrieve the relevant chunk from embedded vectors?

• Given a word/query, how do you determine which embedding or chunk it belongs to?

• Which vector databases have you worked with?

• How are embeddings generated, and why are they useful?

• Which similarity metrics would you use for retrieval? (Cosine similarity, Euclidean distance, etc.)

• How would you improve retrieval quality if irrelevant chunks are being returned?

• What is the difference between chunking strategies, and how does chunk size affect RAG performance?

Final Thoughts
Overall, I found the interview well-structured and enjoyable. It offered a nice balance between traditional data structures and algorithms and modern AI discussions.
I hope this walkthrough gives you a better idea of what to expect.

If you found this helpful, feel free to follow along. I write about software engineering, AI, interview experiences, hackathons, internships, and career growth — sharing what I learn as I build in public. 🚀
